Hey everyone. I didn't know where to post this, or if I'm asking for advice or venting. That is to say, any kind of response is welcome, both advice and general statements, I don't mind. 

So I'm (M 23) probably the black sheep of the family. Not exactly how you might expect though. I'm in good shape, have a decent job, a house, I recently got married, get along great with my wife(F 21).

I grew up to a family who were and are, extremely poor. Most people don't really believe or fully understand what it means when a person who grew up poor says that. It feels like such an understatement. To be clear, poor as in "I'm shocked CPS didn't take me away" poor, no food sometimes and parents struggling to keep minimum wage jobs or a car poor. Maybe what is commonly called redneck or "white trash". How I am not that way and support my wife at age 23 is a whole other story for a different time. I have a strong mountain man streak still of course 🤠

To make things more complicated I'm a black sheep because I'm probably the only person in the family who is not suffering a severe metal health diagnosis. My brother is schizophrenic, sister and mom severe bi polar, grand parents live in their car due to schizophrenia causing unlivable disillusions. We live in rural Appalachia. More things like drugs and lack of opportunity are at play with them too.

My family rely on me in a way that most people would feel inappropriate, but to me I just can't tell. Maybe the trauma bonding from years of growing up like that, maybe guilt. I give all of them rides to and from their jobs. I know this sound untenable because I don't even live in the same town as them but the guilt of knowing I can, and if I don't they'll have no money and nothing else as a result kills me. This means I wake up early, and go to bed late, I also have to squeeze in 8 hours of work in there too. 

The toilet broke at their house a few months back. I showed them a how-to video and the ordered them the part. I learned that months later it was still broken. I fixed it in 5 minutes after going there. Family holidays happen wherever I live. And I cook them. I recently saw the movie Hillbilly Eligy and cried buckets. I was thinking "man, I lived this, I LIVE this now". My wife had never seen me in such a state, the fact that she didn't see what I saw (how could she of course not her fault) made he feel so alone. It's hard when you can't lean on family for anything, and no one else understands. The pressure of providing for them, and providing for my wife at such a young age is a lot. 

People say "cut them off". Those words hurt me. Most people just can't relate to what it feels like. Imagining holding your mom or dads hand on a cliff side and someone says "let go, it's hurting your arm". It's feels so normal to me in some ways. Maybe you gathered this already but I never had a real childhood. I have been an adult so long as I have had needs you could say. 

Now that I'm married my wife may be the most patient person on this earth. I have no idea how she handles having a husband practically married to his family, she meant my mother the car I got for her to use when hers broke down. This was in the days when I thought my family might be able to get or maintain a car. Eventually it ran out of gas and I went and picked up the gasless car, full of trash and ciggeretes butts from the highway. but I know this is too much for my wife despite her support. She needs space, she needs to see me, she needs a husband. I just don't know how to handle it all.

I'm not the type to really post, it write about my problems. I'm a blue collar man who is used to just swallowing things. Carrying everything as best I can. I find it hard to communicate it to my wife. I can tell she wants me to, but I'm afraid she'll loose confidence in me. 

**td;lr 

My(M 23) family (parents, siblings) rely on me a great deal. They struggle with poverty, drugs and mental health. It puts strain on my relationship with my wife(21 F) of 1 year. Guilt pulls me in every direction trying to navigate this.
response_post:
Thank you for sharing so openly about what you’re experiencing. It takes a lot of courage to reflect so honestly on your family, your upbringing, and the challenges of carrying these responsibilities as a young adult and new husband. It’s clear from your words that you care deeply for your family and for your wife, and you’re doing your best to hold everyone together, even while carrying a heavy load.

It can be difficult to find your footing in adulthood when you never really had the chance to experience being a child—when you’ve always had to be the one others depend on. The sense of duty, and the guilt when you imagine stepping back, are entirely understandable given your family’s circumstances and the role you’ve filled for so long. Many people from similar backgrounds can relate to that "arm aching while hanging on" feeling—where letting go seems unthinkable even if you’re suffering.

One thing I notice is how self-aware you are: you recognize the demands on you, the impact it has on your own life and marriage, and even how your instincts to “swallow it all” can make it harder to connect with your wife. That’s a powerful place to start from. Sometimes, the survival skills we learn growing up—like always pushing through, never asking for help, or carrying family burdens—eventually become too heavy, especially as our responsibilities change and grow.

Here are a few things you might consider as you sort through these dilemmas:

**1. Honoring your own needs doesn’t mean abandoning your family.**  
Finding balance is not about “cutting off” your loved ones or failing them. It’s about recognizing your limits and that you are also worthy of care, rest, and happiness. For someone who’s always been the helper, learning to set boundaries can provoke guilt—but boundaries can also be a form of love: they can help relationships stay healthy over the long term.

**2. Communication with your wife**  
While talking about your feelings and worries may feel risky—especially if you fear she’ll lose confidence in you—honest communication often has the opposite effect. Sharing your struggles can increase intimacy and help her understand you even more deeply. She likely wants to support you, not fix you, and being vulnerable might bring you closer.

**3. Acknowledge the power of small steps**  
It can feel like your family’s needs are overwhelming, and any change must be all-or-nothing. In reality, small changes can improve your situation without upending everything. For example, are there tasks you can reduce or delegate, or are there services in your state or community that might help with transportation, food, or other needs? Even considering a schedule where you support your family less frequently might give you and your wife some much-needed breathing room.

**4. Support for you**  
Carrying intergenerational trauma, poverty, and high family needs can weigh heavily. It might help to connect with a counselor, therapist, or support group (even online, especially if you’re in a rural area) who understands family caretaking, codependency, or being a “parentified child.” Even if you’re used to holding it all in, having a place to talk—just for you—can make a difference.

**5. Reflecting on your values**  
You are clearly guided by compassion, loyalty, and a strong work ethic. These are strengths. Sometimes, taking time to think about your personal values—what you want life, marriage, and family to look like in five or ten years—can help you make decisions that are aligned with your deepest beliefs, not just driven by guilt or burnout.

Above all, be gentle with yourself. You’re doing the work of several people, at a young age and under circumstances most people can’t imagine. It’s not merely “blue-collar toughness” to reach out for guidance or to make choices that are sustainable for you and your own family’s future.

This situation is complicated, and there are no easy or universal answers. If it ever feels like too much, it’s okay to seek out local resources, helplines, or professional advice to help you navigate your options. You deserve support, too—even if you’re used to being the strong one.

You’re not alone in feeling this, even if it sometimes seems so. You and your wife both deserve care and peace—and it’s okay to look for small ways to make space for both your families and your own wellbeing.

response_post:
First, I'm really glad you have reached out and shared so openly about your situation—it's clear you've given this a lot of thought, and these are big, deeply personal questions about what will make you feel content and fulfilled in life. Navigating core differences like this in a relationship is tough, and it’s understandable to feel conflicted when there’s a lot of love and connection between you two.

It sounds like you and your boyfriend have explored your wishes honestly, which is already a sign of a healthy partnership. What you each envision as a “fulfilled life” seems to come from very authentic places, but those visions are indeed quite different. Neither one is “better” or “stranger”—they just reflect your unique needs and values.

A few things might help as you reflect further:

**1. Needs versus Compromises:**  
Take some time to explore, gently, which of your needs feel *essential* for your long-term happiness, versus which you’d be open to compromise on. Sometimes writing about what truly makes you feel alive, hopeful, or at peace can clarify the distinction between a passing desire and a core value. 

**2. Your Relationship with Change and Stability:**  
You mention feeling curious about whether you’re “the weird one ready to trade a good relationship for novelty.” It could be helpful to reframe this: wanting to explore and experience new cultures isn’t a flaw—it’s a part of who you are. Neither stability nor adventure holds a monopoly on happiness; it’s about knowing what grounds and inspires *you.*

**3. Fear of Regret:**  
It can help to gently imagine yourself a few years down the line, in each scenario: remaining in your boyfriend’s hometown, or moving to a new city. Which scenario holds more fear for you: the risk of losing the relationship or losing the chance to explore? Regret can come from both action and inaction, so what feels like the “lighter” risk for you?

**4. Ideal Lives and Reality:**  
Sometimes, our visions for the future evolve. Is there *any* overlap between your dreams—perhaps a city that’s new to both of you? Would he consider a trial period somewhere else together, or is he truly deeply rooted? Exploring all options, even those that seem unlikely, can sometimes lead to surprising understandings or compromises.

**5. Self-Judgment and Acceptance:**  
If you notice you’re judging yourself harshly for wanting change, try to offer yourself the compassion you might give a friend in your shoes. It’s normal to struggle when your desires drift from someone you love. It doesn't make you selfish or ungrateful; it means you're thoughtfully considering what you need to feel fulfilled.

**6. Outside Perspective:**  
Talking these feelings through with a counselor, trusted mentor, or even a dedicated friend can provide perspective. Sometimes, just articulating the layers of emotion and fear can bring more clarity.

Ultimately, this is a deeply personal crossroads and only you can know what is right for you. There’s no right or wrong decision—just the one that best aligns with your values and your sense of what will help you grow and thrive. Whatever you choose, you’re not alone in struggling with this kind of decision, and you’re deserving of a life that feels both loving and, in your own way, adventurous.

If it feels safe to do so, continuing open conversations with your boyfriend might also help as you both navigate this—sometimes our “perfect pictures” shift when we look at them together, with empathy.

Take gentle care of yourself as you process this. You’re doing important, brave work in asking these questions.

response_post:
Hello, I'm really glad you reached out and shared what's going on for you. It sounds like you're at a significant crossroads in your life, with multiple changes happening at once—managing the end of a relationship, considering a big move, and starting a new job. That’s a lot for anyone to digest, and it’s very understandable you’re feeling torn.

First, I want to validate the courage it takes to approach this breakup thoughtfully, especially since you mentioned living with Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria (RSD). It makes sense that you’d anticipate this being incredibly painful. Ending relationships, especially when we live together, creates a real sense of upheaval and loss—but it’s also a sign of self-awareness that you want to avoid jumping straight into another relationship and give yourself the space to process and grow.

When it comes to deciding between staying in Scotland or moving back to Ireland, it can help to gently explore a few areas:

### Emotional Support & Self-Care

After a breakup, having a reliable support system can make a tremendous difference while you heal. You mentioned your family and friends are in Ireland and that you’d feel well-supported there—even if it feels like a temporary “step back.” Choosing to prioritize your mental health and accept support isn’t a regression; it’s a form of strength and care for your future self. There's nothing wrong (and a lot that's right) about seeking comfort and grounding during upheaval.

### Stability & Future Planning

You’ve worked hard to land a promising job in Scotland, and it sounds like you see it as a foundation for figuring out your next steps in life. Remaining in Scotland—with all the challenge of finding new accommodation—offers some opportunities: independence, potential professional growth, and perhaps a clearer sense of the life you want to build. Of course, the transition will come with its own set of emotional hurdles, especially while adapting to single life in a familiar but perhaps lonelier city.

### Self-Reflection

Given the pain you anticipate with the breakup and your awareness of past patterns (seeking another relationship to “numb” heartbreak), this could be a moment to ask yourself what you most need, emotionally and practically, to move through this period. Is it more important to feel grounded and safe in the care of loved ones, or are you hoping to challenge yourself by carving out more independence, even if it means feeling alone at first? There’s no right or wrong answer—just what feels most supportive to you right now.

### Transitional Solutions

Sometimes, it’s possible to combine these approaches. For instance, could you take a short, structured break back in Ireland right after the breakup, even for a few weeks, to let yourself grieve and recharge before starting your new job in Scotland? Or, could you start your new job, but keep an open mind about moving back if you find the weight of it all too much to manage on your own?

### Final Thoughts

Whatever decision you make, remember that moving forward isn’t always a straight line. Sometimes, what looks like a step back is actually laying a stronger foundation for the future. Take things one day at a time and allow yourself to grieve, heal, and discover what you want at your own pace.

If you find yourself feeling especially low or overwhelmed, consider reaching out to a counselor—either while in Scotland or Ireland—for some additional one-on-one support. You’re not alone in this, and it’s okay to seek all the help you need while navigating this transition.

Wishing you compassion for yourself in the days ahead.

response_post:
Thank you for sharing your story so openly—there’s a lot of courage in being this honest about what you’ve been dealing with. Reading through what you’ve described, it’s very clear you’ve been trying to take care of yourself, exploring various medications, therapy approaches, supplements, and recognizing patterns in your mental health.

It’s important to validate how hard it is to cope with chronic depersonalisation and depression, especially when it feels like you "shouldn’t" feel this way because your life, from the outside, looks stable or successful. The fact that you are still pushing forward, reaching out, and seeking answers shows real resilience.

A few things stand out from what you’ve described that might be worth reflecting on:

**1. DPDR as a Condition:**  
Depersonalisation and derealisation (DPDR) can be incredibly distressing, and often isn’t well understood by people—even some clinicians. It can be a symptom of anxiety, depression, trauma, or sometimes seems to exist more on its own. While grounding techniques are helpful when DPDR is an acute response (like during panic attacks), when it’s more constant, different approaches sometimes help, such as mindfulness-based therapies, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), and some trauma-informed therapies—even if you don’t identify as having classical trauma.  
You might consider, if you haven’t already, looking into therapists or clinics who specialise specifically in DPDR—these can sometimes offer more tailored support than general CBT.

**2. The Role of Medications:**  
It’s clear you’ve given SSRIs a solid try. It’s frustrating when the help comes at too great a cost (the side effects), or seems to "poop out." Sometimes, people with DPDR and treatment-resistant depression will explore other approaches, such as:
- Different classes of medication (not just SSRIs—sometimes SNRIs, novel antidepressants, or medications like lamotrigine or low-dose antipsychotics, depending on symptoms and psychiatric review).
- Psychotherapy that is not just CBT, as above.
- Controlled, evidence-based use of newer treatments—like ketamine infusions or TMS (transcranial magnetic stimulation)—when traditional medications aren’t effective. These are only appropriate with a clinician’s guidance, but may be something to discuss if available in your area.

**3. Medical Contributors:**  
It’s a good step that you’ve checked vitamin levels, and it’s wise to allow a couple months for B12, folate, and vitamin D to build up and make an impact (especially when levels are very low). While not always a "cure," deficiencies can absolutely drag mood down and make dissociation harder to bear.

**4. Patterns of Avoidance and Burnout:**  
You noted a cycle: pushing yourself to "carry on" and act as if everything is fine, burning out, then retreating, and then slowly building back up. This is a hard pattern to break and very common for people with chronic DPDR. Sometimes, treating yourself with the same compassion you’d give a friend—with permission to slow down when you’re struggling—can actually help break the cycle and reduce the pressure you feel to always "perform."

**5. Existential/Philosophical Aspects:**  
DPDR and depression often have a strong existential flavor—questions about who we are, meaning, feeling disconnected from the world or ourselves. Sometimes, working with a therapist who can support existential exploration can help bring more acceptance to the sensations rather than fighting them constantly. Acceptance doesn’t mean giving up hope for improvement, but rather, creates space to be less at war with what you’re feeling in the moment.

**6. Suicidality and What to Do if Things Get Darker:**  
You mentioned times where things have felt extremely dark or even suicidal. Please remember, these thoughts are *not* a reflection of your worth or your failure to “get better”—they are symptoms of a brain under immense stress. If these thoughts become overwhelming, reaching out—whether to a trusted person, a crisis textline, or emergency services—is a strong and important step. You deserve support during those times.

**Some gentle, practical recommendations:**
- Give the vitamin corrections time, and track any changes.
- Consider seeking out a therapist with experience in DPDR *specifically*, or alternative modalities (ACT, mindfulness, existential).
- If you have access, explore a full physical workup beyond standard vitamins (thyroid, metabolic panel, etc.) if that hasn't been done.
- If interested in non-pharmacological experimental therapies (like TMS), research local providers and bring this up with a psychiatrist.
- Continue gentle self-compassion practices—acknowledge that carrying this for so many years is *real work*.

Finally, you are not alone—there is a whole community of people living with DPDR and depression, many of whom eventually find ways to make life work for them (even if it’s not a "100% cure" narrative). Recovery is possible, and while there’s no single golden bullet, continuing to search for what fits *you* is a vital sign of hope.

If it helps, keep documenting your journey, look for small moments of connection or grounding, and don’t hesitate to lean on supports—even when it feels repetitive. Sometimes improvement comes from many small steps over time.

You’re doing more than enough by keeping on and reaching out. If you need resources or want more specific support options, feel free to ask. Take it a day at a time, and know that it’s okay not to have all the answers yet.

response_post:
Thank you for reaching out and for your thoughtfulness here—your compassion and concern for your neighbor’s well-being really come through in your message. Navigating situations like this is never straightforward, especially when you’re trying to balance your values with a genuine desire to support someone experiencing harm.

It can be distressing to witness what sounds like emotional or verbal abuse happening so close by, and your presence and awareness may already have made a difference in that moment. Many people in your position grapple with how to be helpful without overstepping or causing unintended consequences.

Here are some suggestions that might align with your values and provide support:

**1. Discreetly Share Resources:**  
You could consider leaving printed information about local domestic violence resources in a common area, mailbox, or where it’s likely to be seen—perhaps appearing as a general community resource, not obviously targeted. This takes pressure off the person receiving it and helps maintain anonymity.

In Chicago, organizations like **Connections for Abused Women and their Children (CAWC)** or the **Chicago Domestic Violence Hotline (1-877-863-6338)** provide confidential support.

**2. Foster a Neighborly Connection:**  
If you feel comfortable and safe, a simple, friendly "hello" or introduction when you see her alone could begin to offer a sense of neighborly support. Sometimes, knowing there are friendly, nonjudgmental people around can reduce feelings of isolation.

**3. Create Visible Community Awareness:**  
You might talk with your condo association (without naming names) about displaying flyers or resources broadly for everyone in the building. Normalizing conversations about healthy relationships can make it easier for someone to seek help without feeling singled out.

**4. Prioritize Your Own Well-being:**  
Witnessing this can be upsetting and sometimes triggering. Take time to care for yourself, reach out to supportive friends, or connect with community groups that share your values for further ideas and support.

**Important:**  
Even though calling the police doesn’t align with your stance, remember that your safety matters too—sometimes situations can escalate unpredictably. Trust your instincts on what feels safe and ethical for you.

You’re making a difference by being aware and compassionate. If you’d like more info on local resources or ways to get involved in community-based safety networks, I’m happy to help you brainstorm.

response_post:
Thank you for reaching out and sharing what you and your son have been through. It’s clear how much you care about your child’s well-being—and your own. Taking the steps you have to protect both of you is incredibly important, and not easy. It might help to hear that how your son is behaving right now—seeming “happy-go-lucky,” enjoying time at your mom’s house, not bringing up his father directly—is a very common way children respond after being in a stressful or unsafe environment. Kids are remarkably resilient, and often, they are very tuned in to what the adults around them can handle, too.

**Supporting Your Son:**

- **Safety and Stability First:** You’re already providing your son with a sense of safety and predictability that he may not have had before. The fact that he’s not worried about being yelled at or punished for small mistakes is already making a positive difference.

- **Open Door for Questions:** Children are often aware of much more than we realize, but may not have the words or comfort to talk about it directly. Let your son know that any time he has a question about where his dad is or what’s happening, he can ask you and you’ll do your best to answer honestly, in a way a child can understand.

- **Simple, Honest, Age-Appropriate Explanation:** At 8, your son doesn’t need—and may not benefit from—detailed explanations about the legal process or the full scope of the abuse, unless he brings it up. One possible way to explain the situation:
  
  > “We’re going to be staying here for a while because it’s important for us to feel safe and calm. Dad isn’t living at our house right now because the way he was acting was not okay, and it wasn’t safe for us. The grown-ups are working on making sure we can both stay safe. If you have questions now or any time, you can always ask me.”

- **Reassure Consistently:** Keep letting him know that none of what happened was his fault, and that he deserves to feel safe, loved, and cared for. If he brings up memories or questions, listen first, and try to validate his feelings: “That sounded really scary,” or, “It’s okay to feel sad, angry, or confused about it.”

**You Want to Help Your Son Cope:**

- **Normalize His Feelings:** Sometimes kids worry about missing a parent or feeling relieved/frightened about changes. Let him know all feelings are okay, and he can talk or show his feelings whenever he needs.

- **Routine and Predictability:** Keeping a gentle routine (mealtimes, bedtime rituals, activities) will really help him feel safe and know what to expect.

- **Model Self-Care:** Allow yourself time to rest, accept help from family, and consider your own supports—friends, a counselor, or a support group. Kids pick up on how caregivers take care of themselves, which gives them permission to take care of themselves too.

- **Professional Support:** If you have the opportunity, you might consider connecting with a child therapist or counselor experienced with trauma or family changes, even if only for a few sessions. Sometimes kids open up more to a neutral, supportive adult, and it gives both of you more tools as you move forward.

**About Legal and Custody Issues:**

You’re already clear-headed about your next steps, and you’re putting your son’s safety first by pursuing a long-term protective order and supervised visitation. Continue sharing updates with him in gentle, age-appropriate ways as changes become official. Minimize the uncertainty by telling him what he can expect in the near future, and letting him know you’re there for questions as things change.

**You’re Both Already Doing a Lot Right.**

You’ve already made a huge difference for your son. Creating a safe space, listening, and not rushing him to talk about things he isn’t ready for will support his emotional recovery. Trust your instincts—you know your child best. If his behavior shifts (nightmares, new fears, regression, trouble at school), that might be a flag that he needs extra help, but right now his response sounds like the beginning of healing.

If you need more resources or ideas about talking to your son or supporting him, feel free to ask. You’re not alone in this, and things can get better from here.

response_post:
Thank you for reaching out and sharing what you’re going through—it takes a lot of courage to be open about these challenges. It sounds like you’ve been carrying a heavy load lately, both with the feelings about time (chronophobia) returning and with the difficulties you’re facing at home and in planning for university.

Feeling disconnected from your parents, and struggling with motivation and self-worth, can understandably leave you feeling empty or stuck. When several important areas of life—like family support, education, and your own self-perception—are all feeling unsteady, it makes sense that things might feel overwhelming or even meaningless at times.

Here are a few thoughts that might help guide you through this moment:

1. **Acknowledge Your Progress:** You mentioned that your experience with chronophobia is better than before, and that’s an achievement, even if the feelings return from time to time. It shows you have the inner resources to see yourself through difficult thoughts.

2. **Navigating Parental Relationships:** Sometimes, our perception of how others feel about us, especially parents, can amplify feelings of self-doubt or isolation. Is there a way for you to check in with them, even if it’s just a small conversation about how you’re feeling? If that’s not feasible right now, perhaps reflecting on what honesty and care would look like from your side can be grounding, regardless of their reaction.

3. **Coping with University Setbacks:** Running into obstacles with university housing or guarantors is a common hurdle in Germany and it's not a reflection on your value or capabilities. Remind yourself that this is a systemic barrier, not a personal failure. Are there alternative paths or resources you haven’t explored yet? Sometimes student unions, advice centers, or tenant organizations can help brainstorm options.

4. **Exploring Meaning:** When life feels empty, it can be helpful to focus on *small* things that bring you comfort or interest, even if they seem insignificant—music, a meal you enjoy, a YouTube channel, connecting with someone online. Sometimes, meaning doesn’t appear as a big, obvious force, but as tiny sparks that can slowly gather strength.

5. **Compassion Toward Yourself:** Feeling dislike for yourself when you’re struggling is understandable but can be a tough cycle. Would you speak to a close friend with similar challenges the way you speak to yourself? Practicing even small moments of self-kindness—like acknowledging any effort you’ve made today—can gradually help shift that inner voice.

6. **Seeking Connection:** You’re not alone in feeling this way, and sometimes simply reaching out, as you have here, creates a thread of connection that can be built on. If you feel comfortable, you might think about looking for local or online support groups or talking with someone you trust about these feelings.

If you ever find that your feelings become too overwhelming or unmanageable, it’s always a good step to reach out to a mental health professional or a support service in your country. You deserve support and understanding.

Take it a day at a time, and remember that progress isn't always linear. Even taking one small action, like sharing your feelings here, is a step forward. If you want, feel free to write back and share more—sometimes just talking helps lighten the load.

response_post:
First, I want to acknowledge the tremendous effort and perseverance it took for you to complete your gymnasium degree under such challenging circumstances. It’s clear that you have navigated a lot of internal and external obstacles, and it shows genuine strength and resilience to push through when things felt so overwhelming and isolating. That achievement does matter, regardless of how “common” it might seem to others—it represents your hard work and your ability to keep going even when things felt hopeless.

It’s completely understandable that you’re experiencing a mix of emotions—relief, sadness, shame, and uncertainty—especially as you take steps toward reintegrating socially and planning for your future. Transitions like this can bring up a lot of self-doubt, particularly when you compare your experiences to those of your peers. You’re not alone in feeling left behind or “different” after facing periods of isolation; many people who have struggled with mental health or other life disruptions feel similar when rejoining social environments.

The discomfort you feel in conversations with others is valid, and you’re not “behind” or lacking as a person because your experiences don’t match some imagined timeline. Everyone’s path is different, even if it doesn’t always feel that way when we look around. What might help is gently shifting focus from what you believe you “lack” to recognizing and valuing what you do bring: determination, insight, and the unique perspective that comes from overcoming adversity.

The shame you described about your past is something many people struggle with, especially after periods of withdrawal from life. It might be helpful to explore those feelings in a compassionate way, acknowledging the reasons behind your choices and circumstances, rather than seeing them as personal failures. That shame deserves kindness, not punishment.

It’s also ok to take things one step at a time. Socializing and building connections after isolation is a process, and it makes sense that you’d feel anxious—which might manifest as self-doubt in conversations or discomfort when talking about life experiences. You get to set your own pace, and you don’t have to earn friendship or connection by meeting arbitrary life milestones first. You are worthy of relationships *now*, not just in the future after another accomplishment.

If you find that the shame and sadness are overwhelming, it could be helpful to talk things through with a counselor or therapist. Sometimes, having a space to process these feelings can make a big difference, and there are ways to work with these emotions so they don’t hold you back. You might also find peer support groups helpful—there are many people who’ve been through similar experiences of isolation and restarting in adulthood.

Above all, be gentle with yourself. The past few years have taken courage, and you’re continuing to show that same courage by reaching out and sharing your story. You are making progress, even if it feels slow—and growth is possible, in your own way and at your own rhythm.

response_post:
I'm really glad you reached out and are sharing how you’re feeling right now. Being away from home and in a different environment—especially with loved ones who might not know the full picture—can make intense thoughts and emotions feel even more overwhelming and isolating. It sounds like you’ve done a lot already to keep yourself safe, and that takes real strength.

You mentioned having reached out to your mom—sometimes support can take longer than we’d like, but those connections can make a difference. While you’re waiting for a response, here are a few steps that might help you get through this moment:

- **Ground yourself in the present.** Try naming five things you can see in the room, four you can touch, three you can hear, two you can smell, and one you can taste. This technique, called grounding, sometimes helps turn down the intensity of urges.
  
- **Give yourself small distractions.** You might try watching something on your phone or TV (even if it’s just for a short time), doing a puzzle, doodling, or taking a walk if there’s somewhere nearby that's safe.

- **Write out what you’re experiencing.** Sometimes letting those feelings out by journaling or even composing a letter to yourself can provide a bit of relief.

- **Consider telling your grandparents you’re having a tough time.** You don’t have to give them all the details if that feels unsafe or uncomfortable, but just telling them that you’re feeling unwell or having a hard day may help you feel less alone right now. Sometimes having someone close by—even if they aren't aware of everything—can make things feel a little lighter.

- **Think about your reasons for holding on.** Even if they feel small, reminders of why you want to get through this can be very powerful in moments like these.

If your thoughts start to feel unbearable, remember that you don’t have to face them alone. In Poland, there are helplines you can reach out to for immediate support:
- **Centrum Wsparcia** (available 24/7): 800 70 2222  
They have people who can talk in English if you need.

Even if it's hard, these moments will pass—even when your brain tries to convince you otherwise. You’ve made it through before, and you deserve safety, understanding, and relief. If you feel yourself getting closer to acting on those urges, please reach out by calling a helpline or, if needed, going to a local hospital.

That’s impressive, and not everyone in your position would have managed to achieve all that, especially after facing so many obstacles.

Feeling like you’ve “missed your shot” at relationships is a thought that often comes from comparing yourself to others or to an imagined timeline of how things “should” be. It’s important to remember that meaningful connections and relationships can happen at any stage in life—there’s no cutoff or deadline. While it’s common in your early twenties to worry about falling behind or missing out, attraction and compatibility depend on much more than early experiences. Many people don’t have serious relationships until later, sometimes for the same reasons you mention.

It also sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pain related to your past—loneliness, bullying, and isolation due to both external circumstances and internal struggles. It might be helpful to practice some self-compassion here. We can be so hard on ourselves, but remembering that you did the best you could in those moments, with the resources and support you had, can be a powerful step toward accepting your past.

In terms of the ongoing feeling of loneliness and longing for connection, it might be helpful to look at ways you can gently expand your social world at a pace and in environments that feel right for you. For example, while dating apps and direct approaches didn’t lead where you hoped, sometimes activities that prioritize shared interests—like hobby groups, language classes, or volunteering—create deeper, less pressured opportunities for connection. It can also help to focus on building friendships, as close platonic relationships often naturally lead to wider social circles and eventually to romantic ones.

Living with OCD and social anxiety can make these steps feel daunting, so it’s okay to go slowly and to celebrate small wins. Sometimes, processing these feelings and experiences further with a professional—or even in a support group—can bring more perspective and support. This could be especially helpful as you navigate feelings of grief about the past and hope for the future.

Lastly, it’s completely normal to feel triggered by seeing couples together—it reminds you of something you want, and that hurts. Allow yourself to notice that feeling, acknowledge it, and then gently bring your attention back to what you value in your own life, which sounds rich with growth and potential.

Be patient and kind with yourself. Your journey is far from over, and there are many ways to experience love and connection ahead. You’re not alone in feeling this way—many people have been where you are and have found fulfilling relationships later on. Keep reaching out and taking small steps toward connecting with others.

If you feel up to it, you might consider talking more with someone you trust, or a counselor, about these feelings. Sometimes just having a space to talk it through regularly can make a surprising difference.

You’re doing really well, and better days are ahead—don’t lose hope.

First, please remember that your feelings—fear, sadness, confusion—are understandable, especially when someone you care about is struggling in such a serious way. It’s normal to feel overwhelmed and powerless in this situation, and it might even be scary to not know exactly what’s happening with her.

It’s also important to notice how difficult the situation has become for you. Long-distance relationships add their own stresses, and when friends pull away, it can feel like the support you need just isn’t there. Right now, it’s as though you’re trying to be strong for everyone, including your girlfriend, but you don’t have someone to take care of you in return.

Even though things are very hard, a couple of things might be helpful going forward:

- **Talk It Out:** Is there an adult—maybe a family member, a teacher, or a school counselor—you could talk to about your own feelings or about your concerns for your girlfriend? You shouldn’t have to carry this alone, and adults you trust can sometimes help in ways we wouldn’t expect.

- **Take Care of Yourself:** Your mental health matters too. Make sure you find ways to rest, eat, and do small things that make you feel a bit better. Music, drawing, or even just talking to someone who listens can help lighten the emotional load.

- **Reach Out to Others:** Even though your friend group is quieter right now, maybe try reaching out to one friend you trust, even just to share a meme or say you’re having a tough day. People sometimes step back for their own reasons, but it doesn’t always mean they don’t care.

- **Stay Connected Safely:** If you get to talk to your girlfriend, remind her that you care, that she’s not alone, and that there are adults whose job it is to help (even if things seem broken sometimes). Encourage her to keep communicating with people who can support her there, as hard as that may be.

This is a really tough moment in both your lives. Being there for someone you love is important, but make sure you also find moments to look after your own wellbeing. If you ever feel like it’s just too much, please reach out to an adult or a mental health service in your area, even just to talk. No one has to go through this kind of pain by themselves.

response_post:
Thank you for sharing so openly about your experience—these feelings of being misunderstood and isolated can be really heavy to carry. It sounds like you’re navigating a lot of self-doubt and frustration about how others perceive you, and you’re also noticing that your background and social style might set you apart in ways you hadn’t anticipated.

Social differences like quietness, formality, or even just having a different accent can sometimes create a sense of “otherness” in a group, especially if most people gravitate toward a certain style of interaction. It’s important to remember that being quieter, reserved, or socially anxious doesn’t make you unlikeable or unworthy of connection. Sometimes, people react with discomfort or distance simply because what’s familiar feels “easier,” not because you’ve done anything wrong.

Adjusting to new social environments—particularly when your experiences or upbringing are quite different from those around you—can be a slow and challenging process. It’s understandable that you might feel self-conscious if you’re not picking up on cues right away, but that doesn’t mean you’re incapable of building connections or that you’re destined to be on the outside.

You might find it helpful to take small steps to connect with others at your own pace. Sometimes, even sharing a little about your background ("I went to a really formal school, so I might seem a bit reserved at first!") can help demystify any differences people are sensing and put both you and them at ease. You could also try seeking out spaces—online or offline—where people have more diverse backgrounds or styles, and where you might find others who relate to your experiences.

When self-critical thoughts show up (“I’m so unlikeable,” “Everyone dislikes me,” etc.), it can help to gently challenge them by asking: *Is that always true? Are there moments when things go okay?* Notice any small positive interactions, and remind yourself that it’s okay not to click with everyone—it doesn’t mean you’re broken or deficient.

You might also benefit from support to work on your social confidence and self-compassion. Therapy can provide a safe, non-judgmental space to process these feelings, practice new skills, and gain a more balanced view of yourself.

You deserve kindness, both from others and from yourself. Even if it feels like you’re on the outside now, there *are* people who will value you as you are. Building self-acceptance and seeking out those who appreciate your unique qualities could make a real difference over time. If you’d ever like to explore more coping strategies or ways to navigate these situations, know that’s absolutely an option.

You’re not alone in feeling this way, even if it sometimes seems that way. If you want, I can share some resources or practical tips on social anxiety or adjusting to new environments. Let me know if you’d find that helpful.

Due to me being quiet, it seems that everybody around me makes up terrible and untrue things about me. Some that I hear a lot are "school shooter", "kidnapper", and (my personal favorite) "The Riddler from The Batman". I have never really understood why. Isn't being called a school shooter what can cause one? It's not like I would even want to hurt anybody around me.

This has completely demotivated me from making friends at all. I fear that I'll be seen as a creep even if I try to socialize. It's as if I'm not even seen as another person by them.

When it comes to the rare few I have talked to, they don't even know my name. They refer to me as "Tyrone", "Lebron", or some other random name. Sometimes I'm just reduced to "white boy". I get that it's all light hearted, but it's pretty damn demeaning. I'd like to speak up about it, but I fear I'll just be shut down instantly with how kids my age are.

There are a few kind people who have taken the time to actually understand who I am. The majority of them are women. However, any time I am speaking with them, somebody always has to say something like "W Rizz" (even though I'm talking to them completely platonically) and it just makes things awkward. response_post:
Thank you so much for sharing what you’re experiencing. It takes a lot of courage to be honest about how hard things feel right now, especially when it seems like you’re surrounded by people who don’t truly see or understand you. What you’re going through sounds incredibly isolating, and it’s completely understandable that you’d be feeling hurt and frustrated.

First, please know that struggling socially in high school is a lot more common than it seems. Many people feel like outsiders or that they aren’t accepted for who they are—especially during these years, which are full of changes and uncertainty. The way you’re being treated is unfair and painful, and even if others try to frame it as “joking,” it doesn’t mean you shouldn’t be affected by it.

It sounds like a lot of your classmates are making assumptions based solely on stereotypes or on the fact that you’re quiet by nature. There’s absolutely nothing wrong with being quiet or reserved. People have different ways of interacting with the world, and it doesn’t make anyone less valuable or less worthy of respect.

Some practical steps might help you cope with your environment:

1. **Find Safe Spaces and Supportive People:** Although you mention there are only a few people who seem to understand you, even one or two genuine connections can make a big difference. Is there a club, extracurricular, or online community where people are more accepting? Sometimes shared interests can be a bridge to friendships outside of the typical social hierarchy of school.

2. **Small Steps in Socializing:** If you ever want to practice being more vocal, it can help to start small—maybe greeting someone in class, asking a classmate about homework, or joining a quiet group activity. The idea isn’t to completely change your personality, but to give yourself some positive experiences with connecting to others. And remember, it’s more than okay to be selective about who you open up to.

3. **Boundaries and Self-Advocacy:** The way people refer to you or make jokes at your expense isn’t right. Speaking up for yourself can be scary, but it’s okay to let someone know if something makes you uncomfortable—even just with a simple, “I’d prefer if you called me by my name.” You never owe it to anyone to laugh along with demeaning comments.

4. **Talk to a Trusted Adult:** If this continues to feel overwhelming, or if the bullying is affecting your mental health, consider reaching out to someone at school—like a counselor, trusted teacher, or school psychologist. You don’t have to face this alone, and sometimes adults can help shift the environment or address unfair treatment in ways that feel safer for you.

5. **Self-Compassion:** It’s easy to internalize others’ judgments and start believing there’s something wrong with you. Please try to remind yourself that you are worthy of respect as you are, and that there is nothing wrong with preferring quieter or deeper interactions. It’s also okay to feel upset about being treated poorly. Taking time for things that bring you peace or self-care—reading, music, creative projects—can help recharge you and remind you of your worth outside of school.

It may not change overnight, but high school is not the entirety of your life, and people’s perspectives often mature over time. Your feelings matter, and you deserve to be seen as the whole, valuable human being you are.

If you ever find that your sadness or sense of isolation is becoming overwhelming or leading to thoughts of harming yourself, please reach out for support—whether it’s a counselor, a helpline, or a trusted adult in your life. You aren’t alone in this, and there are people who care and want to help.

Would you like some ideas for joining new activities, or ways to respond when people say things that make you uncomfortable? Or maybe talk more about ways to build self-confidence in hostile environments? Whatever you need, I’m here to help you explore it.

(M30). Have had OCD, anxiety, health anxiety since I was little.

A year ago I was lying in bed to sleep, it was the middle of the night. Suddenly out of nowhere I find it hard to breathe, I get palpitations and a burning sensation in my chest. I get out of bed thinking I'm having a heart attack. I go to the emergency room and after EKG (ECG), tests, blood test and blood pressure measurement, they send me home.

I contact the local health center as after that episode I started to have irregular heartbeats. The doctors take my blood test, urine test, they do an EKG on me, and all values look good. My doctor concludes that it is anxiety and panic, so I see a psychologist. I have trauma from childhood and the year before (2021) my grandmother passed away suddenly, my grandmother who raised me (basically).

I continued to contact the local practitioner as I could wake up in the middle of the night completely out of breath and a racing heart. I am constantly worried about having heart disease, such as a stroke or heart attack, but the doctors say my heart is normal.

I continued to have heart palpitations several days a week, had constant chest pressure and chest pain and finally got a referral to a heart specialist clinic. There they connected me to machines, did an echocardiogram, ultrasound and I had to perform a test where I cycled while connected to machines. According to the results, my heart is normal.

I keep waking up at night with my breath in my throat, the feeling of someone strangling me, pain in my stomach and pressure on my chest and a constant worry and fear of death. I go back to university in 2 weeks, looking forward to that, but really don’t want this shit to affect my studies and social interactions too much. 

I don’t know what to do. Really. I’m on the edge my family members have had it, they don’t want to hear it and they think I’m crazy. I’ve always been an anxious kid, I’m afraid of dying constantly and I think about that a lot, but since I had that one “major” panic attack, my life has changed completely and I haven’t felt well for over a year now. 

I see a therapist, but not that often, once a month maybe (your typical underfunded Scandinavian free health care office). But I’ll have more appointments in the autumn. But i think about trying CBT.

Also, one thing that gets me to relax is meditation, East Asian calming music and Tibetan bowls. I’m thinking about learning Transcendental Meditation.
response_post:
First, I want to acknowledge how overwhelming and exhausting it must feel to be stuck in this cycle of fear, panic, and constant worry about your health, especially after such a thorough physical evaluation and with a history of anxiety and trauma. And it sounds incredibly isolating to feel like even your family members are running out of patience, when what you need most is understanding and support.

You’ve already handled your situation with a lot of courage: seeking emergency and medical care (multiple times), advocating for specialist referrals, and being open to therapy. That’s no small thing—many people struggle just to reach out for help at all.

From what you’ve shared, your experiences do sound very much like panic attacks and health-related anxiety (often called health anxiety or hypochondriasis). It’s common for these kinds of symptoms to become centered on the heart and breathing, because those systems are so responsive to stress and perceived threat, even when all medical tests are clear. That’s not your fault—the mind and body are so tightly connected, and triggers like grief and trauma can make everything feel more raw and immediate.

It’s also not uncommon to get “stuck” in a cycle of health anxiety after a major panic attack, especially if it was sudden and scary. Panic attacks can leave a real impact—they often make people hyper-aware of bodily sensations and worried about their significance, especially when those sensations recur at night, when you’re less distracted.

Here are a few ideas to consider based on what you’ve described:

**1. Increasing Structure and Support**
It’s great that you’ll have more frequent therapy appointments in the autumn. If you feel safe and able, consider letting your therapist know just how intense and disruptive these symptoms have become. Sometimes more frequent sessions or additional forms of support can be requested if things become unmanageable or start affecting your studies and daily functioning.

**2. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)**
CBT is well-supported for panic attacks, OCD, and health anxiety. If you haven’t yet done structured CBT for these issues—especially the parts that deal with thoughts about health fears and panic—you might find it extremely helpful. There are also self-guided CBT resources and digital programs, some of which are free or low-cost, that can be used in between therapy appointments.

**3. Mindfulness and Acceptance-Based Skills**
It’s fantastic that meditation and calming music help you. Learning more formalized mindfulness or acceptance-based approaches (like Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction or Acceptance and Commitment Therapy) can strengthen your ability to notice these sensations and thoughts without automatically reacting with fear. Transcendental Meditation is one option—consistency in any type of mindful or meditative practice is more important than the specific tradition.

**4. Understanding the Panic-Avoidance Cycle**
It can be helpful to learn about how panic attacks and health anxiety reinforce each other—the more we scan or worry about symptoms, the more tension and adrenaline we produce, and the more likely we are to experience uncomfortable sensations. Breaking small pieces of the avoidance and reassurance-seeking cycle (like gradually reducing how often you Google symptoms or how quickly you seek reassurance) is a CBT skill that pays off over time.

**5. Compassion and Self-Talk**
Feeling misunderstood or labeled “crazy” by others is so painful. It can help to consciously practice self-compassion—reminding yourself that you are not alone, that anxiety is a real and painful experience, and that it is not a personal failing or weakness. There’s nothing crazy about being scared by sudden, overwhelming feelings.

**6. Sleep, Routine, and Grounding**
Waking up at night with panic is common; bodies process anxiety in sleep, too. Sometimes small changes to nighttime routines (limiting screen time before bed, practicing progressive muscle relaxation, keeping to regular sleep hours) can reduce the frequency. If you wake with panic, sometimes it can help to get out of bed, splash water on your face, or sit quietly and ground yourself with things you see or feel, rather than fighting to go back to sleep immediately.

**7. Reaching Out at University**
When you return to university, if your symptoms ever feel like they are interfering too much, consider seeking campus mental health services. Even temporary support groups or short-term counseling might help bridge the gap until your regular therapy becomes more frequent.

You’re already doing so much right. Keep holding onto the activities that bring you relief—even small moments of calm are building the foundation for recovery. Your mind and body have been on high alert for so long; gentle, persistent practice and support can help re-train that response over time.

Finally, remember—you aren’t alone. Many people struggle with similar cycles of anxiety and panic (especially when health fears are involved). None of this is your fault, and it does get better with the right supports and tools. Keep advocating for yourself, and don’t hesitate to bring any changes, no matter how small, to your therapist’s attention. You deserve to feel safe and supported as you move forward.

If you ever feel like things are becoming unsafe or impossible to manage, don't hesitate to reach out for additional help—even between appointments. Your wellbeing matters, and you deserve care and understanding.

You’re not “crazy”—you’re managing something really difficult, and you’re showing a lot of resilience just by reaching out. Please take care.

24M

Hi all, first of all i’m NOT asking for a diagnosis. Been to the GP several times. Just searching for some insights and opinions..

So there’s some things I’ve been struggling with for years. Father died in 2013 of cancer, had very difficult years after in which I didn’t grieve his death. Also had a very manipulative girlfriend from 2013 tot 2018. Been also having OCD since I were a kid.

When that relationship ended I started to develop a cough and bowel issues. I saw that my stools were thin and weird. Saw my GP for both issues, but she assured me it would be nothing. Health anxiety started to get worse and I started getting panic attacks. Years passed and the symptoms didn’t go away. Went to my GP so many times to get reassurance about my health..

Last year in april I moved to a new place with my new girlfriend who is my fiancée today! Around that time I felt a very hard immovable lump in my neck that freaked me out. Started worrying about cancer and convinced myself I had it. Went to my GP who referred me for an echo, but nothing weird. Could be an extra rib they said in the hospital. Anyhow, that lump freaked me out to the MAX and I developed an one sided headache at that time. It’s more of like a head pressure around my right temple that sometimes radiates to the neck. It’s literally always there from waking up to going to sleep. Doesn’t wake me up tho. Developed (in my imagination) one sided weakness on the left side, which suddenly went away. The cough I had for years disappeared out of nothing but now I’m left with this one sided head pressure that’s constantly there for 14 months now. Also since a couple of months occasionally a weird smell. For some hours my smell gets distorted and then out of nothing (usually after sleeping) it resets. Bloods are perfect and did some neuro tests (reflexes, pupils etc) and everything is OK. GP doesn’t see a need for an MRI because if it were a brain tumor she’d likely see something in the tests and the symptoms would’ve gotten waayy worse over the course of a year. Also still left with bowel issues. Never a solid stool, always gassy and stools smell bad. Thought I had blood in my stool but my GP said it was undigested foods. Also a constant pressure in my lower left abdomen. Don’t know what to do now, doc says it’s all because of high amounts of stress I put my body to.

Is it really possible that I have this one sided constant pressure because of something psychomatic for so long? It didn’t get worse over time. Also the bowel movements, could this really be stress for so many years? Doc also said bowel cancer usually gives anemia, which i don’t have. Also have strong depersonalization issues for some years which worsened A LOT the last couple of weeks.. no one notices anything. 

I sometimes think I developed bowel cancer some years ago and last year it metastized to my brain and have it there also since. Is this something realistic or am I not being sober in this? Is my heavy derealization from a tumor or more likely the anxiety and trauma’s. Last weeks everything feels very fake.

I would think.. if it were something so serious, something would’ve happened by now right? The phantosmia, the head pressure (that sometimes gets really mild almost not noticable), (perceived) weakness and pulsating tingling in left side of the body would’ve developed significantly if it were really something right? This derealization is not cool.. want to feel normal again..

Sorry for my english btw, I’m dutch.
response_post:
Thank you for sharing your experience so openly—what you’re describing sounds distressing and exhausting, and it’s understandable to reach out for different perspectives after doing all you can to address your physical health with your GP. You’re certainly not alone in struggling with the overlap between physical and emotional symptoms, or in feeling stuck not knowing what’s behind them.

First, your persistence in seeking medical care and following up with tests, including neurological checks and bloodwork, is important and shows you’re taking your health seriously. It’s reassuring that repeat investigations by your GP and specialists haven’t turned up any signs of a severe medical problem, but I recognize that does little to quiet the ongoing discomfort and worries you’re feeling day after day.

What you describe—longstanding health anxiety, panic attacks, and a history of difficult life events (including your father’s death and the aftermath, and the manipulative relationship)—can all have a significant impact on both body and mind. Chronic anxiety and unresolved grief can “live” in the body in a variety of ways: muscle tension, digestive changes, persistent aches and pains, and sensations that can feel very real and alarming. The gut, in particular, is highly sensitive to stress (so much so that there’s a term for it: the "brain-gut connection"). Years of GI discomfort, changes in bowel habits, and tension headaches can absolutely be driven or worsened by stress and anxiety, even without ongoing conscious awareness of being stressed.

Health anxiety especially can lead you into a cycle where each new symptom triggers worry about serious illness, then the worry itself increases bodily tension and physical sensations, which in turn reinforce the anxiety. This cycle, over time, can become very consuming and can even cause sensations like derealization or depersonalization—common responses when anxiety is overwhelming.

Derealization (that “fake,” disconnected feeling) is not uncommon in people under prolonged stress or those with a history of trauma, and it’s your brain’s way of protecting you from emotional overload. It’s very frightening while it’s happening, but in most cases it is a symptom of your mind’s struggle to cope with ongoing distress, not an indicator of something dire like a brain tumor.

You also mentioned noticing a “phantom smell” occasionally (phantosmia). This, too, can sometimes be seen in people experiencing intense anxiety or migraines, though if it becomes more prominent, it’s always worth reporting new or changing symptoms to your healthcare provider.

The fact that these symptoms have remained largely the same for many months or years and haven’t progressed (and that all medical tests come back within normal ranges) does support what your doctor suggested: that chronic stress and anxiety may be central factors here. It might be validating to know that you are dealing with a real issue, just with a different explanation than what your health anxiety fears.

Many people find that these types of symptoms decrease noticeably when underlying emotional issues are addressed. Working with a mental health professional—especially one who works with anxiety or health anxiety, and with experience in trauma and loss—can offer a safe space to unpack these experiences and learn skills for breaking the cycle of health-related anxiety. Therapies like cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), and mindfulness approaches are all evidence-based for these kinds of concerns. Sometimes, addressing the grief from your father's loss or processing the impact of that past relationship can free up mental and physical resources for healing.

You’ve been through a lot, and being able to voice your doubts here is an important step. If the derealization becomes overwhelming, or if you ever find yourself feeling hopeless or unable to cope, please reach out for help from a professional counselor or therapist. Things can improve—even after years of feeling stuck like this.

You deserve to feel safe and comfortable in your own body again. It’s frustrating that our brains and bodies can create such convincing and distressing symptoms, but healing is very possible, especially with support.

Take care, and thank you again for sharing your story here. You're not alone in this.

seeker_post: Hi, I am a physician leading a highly effective hyperbaric oxygen therapy program for PTSD, currently supported by the Israeli MOD for treatment-resistant veterans. I seek to make this treatment available to US veterans who may need it. How can I contact veteran associations and hyperbaric centers to explore knowledge sharing?
response_post:
First, I want to acknowledge the incredible work you are doing in supporting veterans with treatment-resistant PTSD. It’s clear you’re deeply committed to expanding access to evidence-based care and fostering meaningful change for those affected.

To help facilitate knowledge sharing and potential program expansion in the US, here are some steps you might consider:

**1. Connecting with Veteran Organizations:**
- **Major National Organizations:** Reach out to established groups such as the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA), Veterans of Foreign Wars (VFW), American Legion, Wounded Warrior Project, and Disabled American Veterans (DAV). These organizations often have dedicated contacts for health initiatives and evidence-based treatments.
- **State and Local Veteran Affairs Offices:** Each US state has its own office for veterans’ affairs, which may be interested in innovative treatment modalities.
- **LinkedIn and Professional Platforms:** Consider direct messaging or connecting with leaders of veteran health initiatives on LinkedIn or professional networks.

**2. Hyperbaric Centers and Professional Societies:**
- **Undersea and Hyperbaric Medical Society (UHMS):** This is the primary professional organization in the US. They host conferences, publish guidelines, and foster collaboration. Their website provides contact information and resources.
- **Academic Medical Centers:** Many medical schools associated with VA hospitals or with departments of psychiatry/trauma may house hyperbaric programs.
- **Regional Hyperbaric Centers:** Identify hyperbaric centers through the UHMS facility directory or state medical boards. You can often contact program directors directly.

**3. Sharing Knowledge and Building Partnerships:**
- **Offer to Present Your Work:** Organizations are often open to webinars, grand rounds, or invited talks about new clinical developments.
- **Prepare Supporting Materials:** A summary of your clinical outcomes, published research, and program logistics will help foster credibility and mutual interest.
- **Propose Collaborative Research:** Suggesting joint research or case sharing often creates a bridge for ongoing dialogue.

**4. Leverage Professional Networks:**
- **Conferences and Symposia:** Submit abstracts or attend US-based conferences on trauma, veteran health, and hyperbaric medicine.
- **Peer-Reviewed Publications:** Publishing in international journals can sometimes draw direct outreach from interested US groups.

**5. Consider Legal and Regulatory Requirements:**
- While pursuing these connections, it's helpful to be aware of FDA regulations and the VA’s therapeutic protocols regarding innovative treatments, as this may arise in discussions.

If you'd like, I can suggest specific conferences or platforms where leaders in veteran care and hyperbaric medicine come together. Additionally, if you have published outcomes data or case reports, those can be powerful tools in your outreach.

Your drive to share your expertise with a broader community is admirable, and these steps may help you make meaningful connections to serve more veterans in need.

response_post:
Hey there, thank you so much for sharing your story and being so open about what you’re going through. That takes a lot of courage, especially when things feel overwhelming and lonely.

It sounds like you’re carrying a tremendous amount on your shoulders—navigating young fatherhood, relationship pain, feeling let down by friends and family, financial stress, all on top of past experiences with depression. Feeling hurt, exhausted, or lost in a situation like this is completely understandable. It makes sense that it would feel like too much to handle alone.

Having people you trust turn away or not take you seriously can make you feel even more isolated. That feeling that others just expect you to “man up” or handle it all by yourself is a heavy burden—and it’s not how anyone should have to live. You deserve support, understanding, and care, especially right now.

Even if things feel hopeless at the moment, it’s important to remember that your reactions—feeling broken, sad, or overwhelmed—are valid responses to a tough, unfair situation. And as much as it hurts, feeling this way doesn’t define your worth or your future. You clearly care deeply for your child and are trying your best with the circumstances you’ve been given—those are huge strengths, even if they don’t always feel like it.

If you haven’t already, it might help to talk with a mental health professional—someone whose job it is to listen, support, and help you find ways to manage or lighten some of this weight. Sometimes just having someone on your side who won’t judge or dismiss your feelings can make things feel a little more manageable. If finding a mental health professional feels overwhelming, consider reaching out to a helpline or support group, or asking your doctor for advice—sometimes a small step can be the start of something better.

You don’t have to go through all of this alone, and there are people out there who genuinely want to help, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now. You’ve already taken a brave first step by sharing here.  

Take it one day at a time, and don’t be afraid to reach out again—here or elsewhere. You matter, and there is hope for things to get better.

Sending you strength and compassion.

At 23 years old I moved from Rome to the Netherlands to study for my Master, and I had the time of my life. I made a lot of friends from every possible country, I had been in love, I have discovered a lot about myself. 
Moving from the center of Rome, one of the biggest cities in the world, to such a small country like The Netherlands. What I found there was open-mindedness and obviously a very international and different social setting, which has led me indeed to bond with many different people with different backgrounds and thus likely habits.
Then though, I had to move back to Rome at the end of the experience, but after one year staying here I feel like my life has no purpose here. I was used to a life of new discoveries everyday, I was challenged to change my habits in order to experiment new cultures, but now it all disappeared. How do you manage to go back to the usual routine and the usual neighborhood life? Everybody told me to try and change it, but how can you really, in such an environment? Should I go alone to places and introduce myself?
response_post:
Thank you for sharing your experiences and the way you're feeling right now. It's very natural to feel this sense of emptiness or stagnation after such a vibrant and transformative chapter in your life, especially returning from a period of growth, excitement, and self-discovery. Reverse culture shock—coming home after being immersed in a different environment—can be just as powerful as the initial shock of moving away.

It sounds like what enriched your life in the Netherlands was a sense of *novelty*, *challenge*, and *connection*. Returning home, where things feel more fixed or familiar, can leave you longing for the depth of those social experiences and personal discoveries.

Here are a few gentle suggestions that sometimes help when grappling with this kind of transition:

1. **Honor the Grief and Celebrate What You Gained:** It's valid to mourn what you miss about your life abroad. At the same time, recognizing the skills, strengths, and new perspectives you gained can help you see how you've changed—and that change can be carried forward.

2. **Seek Out Micro-Adventures:** While it may not feel as bold or exciting as living in a new country, sometimes curiosity can be fostered at home. Are there international communities, language exchanges, or cultural events in Rome that you haven’t explored yet? These spaces sometimes attract like-minded people who crave diversity and new connections.

3. **Try the “Go Alone” Experiment:** While it can feel daunting, going alone to events or new places can be an empowering first step. Many people report that those times, though initially awkward, led to valuable connections. Consider joining meetups, classes (cooking, dance, art), or volunteering—places designed for connection.

4. **Reframe Your Perspective on ‘Routine’:** Sometimes, familiarity can feel dull—but it can also provide a foundation from which to launch new experiences. Instead of seeing your neighborhood as unchangeable, perhaps there are ways you can interact with it differently: photography walks, writing, or simply exploring hidden streets.

5. **Stay Connected to Your International Network:** Nurture the friendships you’ve made abroad—perhaps through video calls, planning visits (both ways), or even hosting new friends in Rome, exposing them to your city through fresh eyes.

6. **Set New Challenges:** Think creatively about ways to challenge yourself. Is there a new professional skill or hobby you’re curious about? Setting yourself a project or goal (even if it's outside your comfort zone) can bring back a sense of growth.

7. **Share Your Story:** Perhaps there's a way for you to give back—sharing your experiences with other students preparing to study abroad, or creating content about cultural transition and adaptation, which can help you process your journey and help others at the same time.

Lastly, if you find that the feelings of emptiness linger or are affecting other parts of your life (like mood, motivation, or relationships), consider reaching out to a local counselor or mental health professional. Sometimes, having a safe space to process transitions can be very supportive.

The chapter in the Netherlands may have ended, but the excitement and curiosity it sparked in you can absolutely continue to grow, even at home. Take your time, be gentle with yourself, and allow your journey to keep unfolding.

You're not alone in this—it’s a very human feeling after such a big adventure.

Thank you for sharing how you’re feeling right now—it takes courage to open up, especially during such a long and difficult period.

From what you’re describing, it sounds like you’ve been carrying a lot since losing your job. Not having daily structure or meaningful activities can really take a toll on our sense of purpose and mood. It’s completely understandable that you’re feeling sad and “rotting away” with the way your days have been going.

Even though things are hard right now, there *are* steps you can take that might help bring back some energy and hope, even if they feel small at first:

- **Routine and Structure:** Sometimes, creating a gentle daily routine—even simple things like getting up, showering, or going for a walk at the same time each day—can help anchor us and break up the monotony.
- **Movement:** Physical activity, even light stretches or short walks outside, can have a surprisingly positive effect on mood. Sometimes just being outside or moving your body can help shift things, even a little bit.
- **Connection:** You mentioned living with your girlfriend. Have you had the chance to talk to her about how you’re feeling? Sharing with someone close can help, and you don’t have to manage all these feelings alone.
- **Meaningful Activities:** Are there small activities or hobbies that you used to enjoy before everything changed? Even re-engaging with something small, like listening to music, drawing, reading, or volunteering, can make a difference over time.
- **Professional Support:** If these feelings are getting heavier or you notice them getting in the way of basic daily activities, reaching out for professional support can be really helpful. Norway has mental health services and helplines, and even speaking with your doctor about how you've been feeling is a strong and positive step.

Many people are struggling with similar feelings after so much disruption from the pandemic, and it’s nothing to be ashamed of. Lasting change often starts with small steps—even commenting here is one. Be gentle with yourself, and remember that things *can* shift with time and the right support.

If it feels like too much right now, that’s okay. Sometimes, focusing just on today and what’s within your control can help keep things manageable. And if your sadness ever gets to the point where you feel hopeless or have thoughts of giving up, please reach out—there are people who care and want to help, both professionals and those in your life.

You’re not alone in feeling like this. Sending you empathy and encouragement as you navigate these tough days.
